items.mistake: Using nested Python loops over NumPy array elements for numeric transforms.
items.fix: Rewrite as ufuncs, axis reductions, slice assignment, or boolean masking on the whole array.
items.why: Each loop iteration pays Python dispatch cost; on 100k+ element design matrices this dominates runtime and blocks BLAS-friendly memory access.
items.example: Replace `for i in range(len(x)): y[i] = x[i] ** 2` with `y = x ** 2`.

items.mistake: Assuming broadcasting aligned arrays the way you intended without checking `.shape`.
items.fix: Print or assert shapes before and after the operation; use `reshape`, `[:, np.newaxis]`, or `np.broadcast_shapes` when designing alignments.
items.why: Trailing-dimension rules can silently apply a `(3,)` vector across columns when you meant per-row offsets, corrupting features without an error.
items.example: Adding `bias` of shape `(n_samples,)` to `X` of shape `(n_samples, n_features)` broadcasts along columns — use `bias[:, np.newaxis]` for per-row bias.

items.mistake: Calling `.tolist()` or appending rows in a loop when building a design matrix.
items.fix: Pre-allocate `np.empty((n, d))` or `np.zeros` and fill slices, or stack once with `np.vstack` / `np.concatenate` after collecting arrays.
items.why: Repeated Python list growth forces boxed floats and later conversion back to ndarray, losing contiguous layout and vectorization benefits.
items.example: Build `batch = np.vstack([feat_a, feat_b])` instead of `rows = []; rows.append(...)` in a loop then `np.array(rows)`.

items.mistake: Mixing dtypes inside hot loops so NumPy promotes to wider or object types unexpectedly.
items.fix: Cast once to a single dtype (`astype(np.float64)`) before the vectorized pipeline stage.
items.why: Dtype promotion can create extra temporaries or object arrays that disable fast kernels and inflate memory.
items.example: If `X` is `int64` and you add `0.5`, the result is float — cast `X = X.astype(np.float64)` up front when building a float pipeline.
